title: Tighten path matching and baseline fingerprints

sources.reason: Shared matching and fingerprint helpers used by scope and baseline checks.
Regression coverage for changed-path baseline behavior.
Regression coverage for policy matching behavior.

riskAssessment.reason: Scope and baseline checks must use path semantics that do not overmatch nested files and must distinguish type or mode-only changes.

executionDecision.reason: The matching and fingerprint semantics can be tightened locally and regression-tested.

preReviewWarnings: Keep path matching glob semantics narrow and preserve baseline dirty detection for mode or type changes.

checkpointPolicy.reason: Record a finish-time checkpoint before archiving the matching and baseline fingerprint fix.

acceptance: Path matching no longer lets single-star patterns overmatch deeper nested files.
Baseline dirty fingerprints detect mode or file-type-only differences instead of treating them as unchanged.
Regression tests prove the tightened semantics for scope and baseline comparisons.

intent.problem: The shared matching helper uses fnmatch semantics that can overmatch nested paths, and the baseline fingerprint only hashes file contents, so mode or type-only changes can be missed by scope and dirty-baseline checks.
constraints: Keep the fix local to the shared helper and regression tests.
Do not widen scope matching beyond the documented glob behavior.
Preserve existing callers and contract shapes.
intent.rationale: Shared matching and baseline identity must reflect the real path and file identity so governance checks do not silently miss changes.

scenarioCoverage.scenario: single-star patterns do not match nested descendant files
baseline fingerprints change when file mode or type changes even if file bytes stay the same
existing exact and recursive patterns still behave as expected

restrictedWriteApproval.reason: Set only when a human explicitly approves restricted governance paths.

rollbackNote: Revert this Work Item diff and restore related tests and docs.
